content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 26,
1995
Ms. Iris P.
Yang
McDonough,
Holland and Allen
555 Capitol Mall,
9th Floor
Sacramento, California
95814
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—95-070a
Dear Ms.
Yang:
You have asked for amplification or reconsideration of advice
provided to you in Letter No.
A-95-070 on behalf of City of
Emeryville Councilmember Greg Harper and Planning Conunissioner
Paul Germain under the conflict—of—interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
We previously advised and reaffirm that Councilmember Harper
and Planning Conunissioner Germain must disqualify themselves from
decisions concerning the Chiron Corporation’s proposed expansion
of its business headquarters and biotechnology researãh and
development center (the “Project”)
since their residences in an
artists’ cooperative are located within 300 feet of the Project,
and decisions about the Project would have a material financial
effect on their real property interests.
Facts about the Project
and the artists’ cooperative are as described in Letter No.
A—95—
070.
In your initial advice request you stated that the Project is
of such magnitude that it will obviously have a material financial
effect upward or downward on the value of the artists’ cooperative
property which is adjacent to it.
As discussed in Letter No.
A-95-070,
Mr.
Harper and Mr.
Germain’s interests in the artists’
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections 18000-18954.
All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95804-0807 •
(916) 322-5660


File No.
A—95—070a
Page
2
cooperative where each of them lives are interests in real
property that may be indirectly affected by decisions regarding
the Project.
(Regulation 18702.3.)
Regulation 18702.3(a) (1)
provides that the financial effect of a decision is material as to
real property in which an official has an ownership interest if:
The real property in which the official has an
interest, or any part of that real property, is
located within a 300 foot radius of the boundaries
(or the proposed boundaries)
of the real property
which is the subject of the decision, unless the
decision will have no financial effect, upon the
official’s real property interest.
Therefore, decisions regarding the Project will be considered to
have a material financial effect on Mr. Harper and Mr.
Germain’s
real property interests unless they would have no financial effect
on such interests.
Assuiuing that the Project will affect the fair market value
of the property owned by the artists’ cooperative, you ask whether
any such effect is considered to be a material financial effect on
the officials’ real property interests if the fair market value of
the property has no financial effect on the value at which they
can transfer their shares in the corporation?
You emphasize that the 45th Street Artists’ Cooperative,
a
nonprofit corporation (the “corporation”) that owns the
cooperative, is a 501(c) (3)
corporation, the bylaws of which
establish the value of the shares and the price at which they can
be redeemed when an owner—resident moves.
Under the corporation’s
bylaws, if either Mr. Harper or Mr. Germain were to sell his
shares in the cooperative, the corporation is limited in what it
can pay for the shares.
The price is determined by the amount the
resident—owner paid for the shares, the value of any improvements
installed by the resident—owner, plus an adjustment based on the
consumer price index.
The corporation’s board of directors
decides to whom the shares can be sold.
You argue that the fair
market value of the property has absolutely no effect on the value
of the shares or the price which the owner of the shares can
receive if he or she wants to sell his or her interest in the
corporation.
We disagree with your conclusion that changes in the fair
market value of the artists’ cooperative property would have no
financial effect on the officials’ real property interests for the
reasons stated below.
First,
a change in the fair market value of the artists’
cooperative property as a result of the adjacent Project, might
very likely have some effect on the officials’ ability to resell


File No.
A—95-070a
Page
3
their residences (cooperative shares),
should either of them
decide to move.
Your initial letter acknowledges that as a
practical matter,
Mr. Harper or Mr. Germain probably would not be
able to sell his shares back to the corporation unless the
corporation had a ready and willing buyer on hand to purchase the
shares.
If the Project increases the value of the artists’
cooperative property,
more willing buyers might be on hand.
If,
on the other hand, the Project adversely affects the value of the
artists’ cooperative property, there might be fewer buyers
interested.
This would determine whether the official could sell
immediately when he chooses or needs to move, or whether he is
forced to wait for a period of several months while paying rent or
mortgages on two residences.
Second, if the fair market value of the artists’ cooperative
real property increases, it could have a financial effect on the
officials’ real property interests because the corporation’s
bylaws provide that increases in corporate equity can be used for
improvements to the property.
The corporation’s bylaws in Article
VIII define “corporate equity” as:
the excess of the current fair
market value of the corporation’s real property over the sum of
the current transfer values of all shares or membership interests,
reduced by the principal balance of all outstanding encumbrances
on the corporate real property.
The bylaws state that corporate
equity cannot be distributed to members, but can be used
(a)
for
the benefit of the corporation or improvement of the real property
or
(b)
for expansion of the corporation by acquisition of
additional real property.
If the Project increases the fair market value of the
artists’ cooperative property,
and thereby results in an increase
in corporate equity, the money could be used by the board of
directors of the artists’ cooperative for improvements to the real
property.
Such improvements could include, for example,
repainting or re-landscaping the building, installing a new or
improved security system, installing a ne or improved heating or
air conditioning system for all the units, etc.
If corporate
equity increases and the board of directors decides to make
improvements to the property, the improvements could have a
financial effect on the officials’ real property interests, since
they would not need to pay for similar improvements to the units
themselves.
Similarly, if the Project decreases the fair market value of
the artists’ cooperative real property, the value of the corporate
equity would decline.
It is conceivable then, that necessary
upkeep or improvements to the real property could not be made and
that this, too,
could have a financial effect on the officials’
real property interests.
For instance, they might have to pay to
add additional security,
soundproofing, or insulation to their own


File No.
A-95--070a
Page
4
unit, rather than have the board of directors agree to make this
improvement for all units.
The value of improvements to the units installed by the
resident—owners is included in the formula used to calculate the
transfer price of the shares (i.e., the sale price of the unit).
Therefore,
any increase or decrease in the corporate equity, which
is directly tied to increases or decreases in the fair market
value of the artists’ cooperative property, could have a financial
effect on the officials’ real property interests through the
corporation’s ability to make improvements in the real property.
In addition, although the residents do not have a separate
interest in the common areas of the artists’ cooperative, they are
presumably entitled to the use and enjoyment of these areas
through ownership of their shares.
Under Section 82033 of the
Act, this may constitute a beneficial interest in real property
for the officials if the value of their interest in the common
areas is worth $1,000 or more.
(See Field Advice Letter,
No.
A-
94—106.)
The corporation may make improvements in the
cooperative’s common areas when there is excess corporate equity,
which is directly tied to the fair market value of the property.
I trust this answers your question.
If you have any further
questions regarding this matter, please contact me at 916/322-
5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G.
Churchwell
General Counsel
e w-J
By:
Hyla P.
Wagner
Counsel, Legal Division

5660.
